{{wikidata link|Q11620885}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Katsuragi Hitokotonushi Shrine |Image = [[File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja haiden and icho.jpg|270px]]
Haiden and the lactiferous ginkgo
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |Location = Moriwaki 432, {{jalink|Nara Prefecture|Q131287}}{{jalink|Gose City|Q847334}} |ISO = JP-23 |Latitude degrees = 34|Latitude minutes = 26|Latitude seconds = 43.97 |Longitude degrees = 135|Longitude minutes = 42|Longitude seconds = 42.98 |Enshrined deities = {{jalink|Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|Katsuragi no Hitokotonushi Ōkami}}
{{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|Wakatakeru no Mikoto}} |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|Myōjin Great Shrine}})
Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Alternate name = |Pilgrimage site = |Annual festival = {{jalink|April 5|Q2507}}, {{jalink|September 15|Q2846}} |Rituals = |Map = Japan Nara }} '''Katsuragi Hitokotonushi Shrine''' (かつらぎひとことぬしじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in {{jalink|Gose City|Q847334}}, {{jalink|Nara Prefecture|Q131287}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and under the {{jalink|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|former shrine ranking system}} it was designated a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. It is commonly known as “Ichigon-san” or “Ichikon-ji-san.” The shrine is enshrined facing east on the eastern foothills of {{jalink|Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Q11433560|Mount Katsuragi}}. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following two deitiesShrine history pamphlet.. * {{jalink|Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|Katsuragi no Hitokotonushi Ōkami}} (かつらぎのひとことぬしのおおかみ) – principal deity. * {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|Wakatakeru no Mikoto}} (わかたけるのみこと) – the 21st Emperor Yūryaku. The Meiji-era *Shrine Register* lists the two deities as Kotoshironushi no Mikoto and Wakatakeru no Mikoto, while the *Yamato Shiryō* states that Hitokotonushi is the main deity with Emperor Yūryaku enshrined jointly{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}{{Sfn|奈良県史 5|1989年|pp=449-450}}. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* {{jalink|Register of Deities|Q11064932|Jinmyōchō}}, the shrine has one seat, and appears as “Katsuki-ni-masu Hitokotonushi Shrine,” indicating that it originally enshrined only {{jalink|Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|Hitokotonushi}}{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. This deity is written as “Hitokotonushi” in the *{{jalink|Kojiki|Q813031}}*, “Hito-kotonushi” in the *{{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}*, and “Hito-go-nushi” in the *{{jalink|Nihon Ryōiki|Q1606838|Nihon Ryōiki}}*. He is regarded as a god of {{jalink|oracles}}, who utters both good and ill fortune in a single word, and is still worshiped today as a deity who grants wishes with a single utterance{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. Although no genealogy is recorded in the *Kojiki* or *Nihon Shoki*, the *{{jalink|Sendai Kuji Hongi|Q2716751}}*“Sendai Kuji Hongi,” *Chigi Honki*. identifies Hitokotonushi as a child of {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. [[File:Tennō Yūryaku.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{Center|{{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} and the great boar of {{jalink|Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Q11433560|Mount Katsuragi}}}}]] Regarding Hitokotonushi, a well-known encounter tale with {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} appears in both the *Nihon Shoki**Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Yūryaku, 4th year, 2nd month. and the *Kojiki**Kojiki*, section on Emperor Yūryaku.. According to both accounts, while Emperor Yūryaku was hunting in the mountains of Katsuragi, Hitokotonushi (also written as Hito-kotonushi), who appeared in the same form as the emperor, emerged and competed with him in the hunt{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. In the *Kojiki*, however, the emperor offers a great sword, bow and arrows, and many garments to the deity and worships him, portraying Hitokotonushi as superior, whereas in the *Nihon Shoki* the emperor makes no such offerings and the two are depicted as nearly equal in status{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. Since the *Kojiki* is considered more archaic, its account is thought to reflect the political circumstances when the {{jalink|Katsuragi clan|Q11620908}}—regarded as the clan responsible for the worship of Hitokotonushi—held strong power as imperial in-laws, whereas the *Nihon Shoki* reflects a later period when the Katsuragi clan declined and the status of Hitokotonushi diminished{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}*Shinpen Nihon Koten Bungaku Zenshū 3: Nihon Shoki (2)*, Shogakukan, 2004 (JapanKnowledge edition), pp. 158–160.. In later periods, in Heian-era works such as the *{{jalink|Nihon Ryōiki|Q1606838|Nihon Ryōiki}}**Nihon Ryōiki*, volume 28. and the *{{jalink|Konjaku Monogatari-shū|Q1507085}}**Konjaku Monogatari-shū*, volume 11, story 3., Hitokotonushi is described as being conscripted by {{jalink|En no Gyōja}} (En no Ubasoku / En no Ozunu) to build a bridge between {{jalink|Mount Kinpu|Q10563986}} and Mount Katsuragi, and even bound by a curse after incurring En no Gyōja’s anger—indicating a further decline in divine authority{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. It is also said that during this forced labor, Hitokotonushi hid the ugliness of his face and worked only at night, not by day; inspired by this tale, {{jalink|Matsuo Bashō|Q5676}} composed a poem in *Oi no Kobumi* (see “{{jalink|#Appearances in works||Appearances in works}}”){{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. [[File:Tosa-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{jalink|Tosa Shrine|Q704890}} ({{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}{{jalink|Kōchi City|Q200251}})}}{{small|Currently enshrines {{jalink|Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|Hitokotonushi}} and {{jalink|Ajisukitakahikone||Ajisukitakahikone no Kami}}.}}]] According to the *{{jalink|Shoku Nihongi|Q611399}}*, entry for the 8th year of {{jalink|Tenpyō-hōji|Q1346307}} ({{jalink|764|Q31163}})*Shoku Nihongi*, Tenpyō-hōji 8 (764), 11th month, day 7., a deity called “Takakamo no Kami,” who had competed in hunting with Emperor Yūryaku, had been exiled to {{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330}}, but in that year was re-enshrined in his original place{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. However, one passage cited in the *{{jalink|Shaku Nihongi|Q2710483}}* from the *Rekiryoku* states that in Tenpyō-hōji 8, Hitokotonushi was brought back from Tosa and enshrined in Katsuragi{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. Another excerpt from the *Tosa Province Fudoki* cited in the *Shaku Nihongi* notes that the deity of Takakamo Taisha in Tosa (present-day {{jalink|Tosa Shrine|Q704890}}) is Hitokotonushi no Mikoto, though another tradition identifies him as {{jalink|Ajisukitakahikone|Q506610|Ajisukitakahikone no Mikoto}}. Thus, the sources show confusion between Hitokotonushi and Takakamo no Kami (Ajisukitakahikone no Mikoto, enshrined at {{jalink|Takakamo Shrine|Q11673295}}){{Sfn|平林章仁|2013年|pp=235-253}}. Additionally, due to similarities in name and their nature as oracle deities, some theories identify Hitokotonushi with {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi no Mikoto}}{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. === History === The date of foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition holds that the current site is where Hitokotonushi manifested. In the {{jalink|Six National Histories|Q1151168|official histories}}, it is recorded that in the 3rd year of {{jalink|Kashō|Q1373157}} ({{jalink|850|Q30364}}), the divine rank of “Katsuki Hitokotonushi no Kami” was raised to Senior Third Rank, and that in the 3rd year of {{jalink|Ten’an (Japanese era)|Q1075611|Ten’an}} ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) in the first month, it was promoted to Junior Second Rank, Second Class of Merit{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. In September of the same year, the first year of {{jalink|Jōgan (Japanese era)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} (859)*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, Jōgan 1 (859), 9th month, 8th day (see {{Harvnb|神道・神社史料集成}})., envoys were dispatched to present offerings to Hitokotonushi and other deities in prayer for rain and wind{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. In the 5th year of {{jalink|Shōryaku|Q1324858}} ({{jalink|994|Q24114}}), fourth month*Honchō Seiki*, Shōryaku 5 (994), 4th month., offerings were again presented, with members of the {{jalink|Nakatomi clan|Q1198447}} serving as envoys, in response to epidemics and fires{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* {{jalink|Register of Deities|Q11064932|Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274}} ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), the shrine is listed in Yamato Province, {{jalink|Katsukami District|Q11620858}}, as “Katsuki-ni-masu Hitokotonushi Shrine, Myōjin Taisha, Monthly and New Harvest Offerings,” indicating its status as a {{jalink|Myōjin Taisha}} and that it received offerings from the court at the {{jalink|Monthly Rite|Q11516161}} and {{jalink|New Harvest Festival|Q11501518}}{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. Subsequent history is unclear. In the past, there was a temple called Ichigon-ji to the southeast of the shrine as a {{jalink|jingū-ji|Q1074971}}, but it has since been abandoned{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6), the shrine was ranked as a {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}} under the {{jalink|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534}}, and in March {{jalink|1883|Q7816}} (Meiji 16) it was promoted to {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}{{Sfn|明治神社誌料|1912年}}. === Divine ranks === * {{jalink|Kashō|Q1373157}} 3 ({{jalink|850|Q30364}}), October 7: Senior Third Rank (*{{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}*)*Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku*, Kashō 3 (850), 10th month, day 7 (see {{Harvnb|神道・神社史料集成}}). – recorded as “Katsuki Hitokotonushi no Kami.” * {{jalink|Ten’an (Japanese era)|Q1075611|Ten’an}} 3 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}), January 27: Promoted from Senior Third Rank, Second Class of Merit to Junior Second Rank, Second Class of Merit (*{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}*)*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, Jōgan 1 (859), 1st month, 27th day (see {{Harvnb|神道・神社史料集成}}). – recorded as “Katsuki Hitokotonushi no Kami.” === Shrine grounds === [[File:Katusragi-hitokotonushi-jinja icho.jpg|thumb|180px|{{center|Lactiferous ginkgo}}]] * Main hall – Rebuilt in {{jalink|1876|Q7766}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 9). One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} style, with a copper-sheet roof{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. * Worship hall – In front of the hall stands an ancient {{jalink|ginkgo|Q43284}} tree (the lactiferous ginkgo), said to be 1,200 years old and regarded as a sacred tree. * Offering hall * Shrine office * Spider Mound – A mound based on legends of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}’s subjugation of the {{jalink|earth spiders|Q2063089}}{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. * Great Rite Memorial Hall * Statue of {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} * Hōrai Stone * Turtle Stone === Auxiliary and branch shrines === * Jingū Kōgō Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} * {{jalink|Tenman Shrine}} – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005}} * {{jalink|Hachiman Shrine}} – Enshrined deities: {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|Hondawake no Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803|Okinaga Tarashihime no Mikoto}} * Ichikishima Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|Ichikishimahime no Mikoto}} * Hitokoto {{jalink|Inari Shrine|Q514480}} – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ukemochi|Q2280323}} * Haraedo Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Seoritsuhime|Q13579836}} no Mikoto * {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Shrine|Q11381857}} – Enshrined deities: {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Sokotsutsuo no Mikoto, Nakatsutsuo no Mikoto, Uwatsutsuo no Mikoto}}, Okinaga Tarashihime no Mikoto * Okumiya – About 50 meters west of the main hall, a {{jalink|iwakura}} is enshrined as the inner shrine. File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, Kame Stone 01.jpg|Turtle Stone File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, Kumo Stone 01.jpg|Spider Mound File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, 07.jpg|First torii gate Katusragi-hitokotonushi-jinja keidaisha-2.jpg|Shrines within the grounds Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, Auxiliary Itokoto-Inari Shrine 01.jpg|Hitokoto Inari Shrine === Festivals === List of annual festivals held at Katsuragi Hitokotonushi Shrine. * New Year’s Day Festival (January 1) * Setsubun Festival (around February 3) * Spring Grand Festival (April 5) * Inari Shrine Festival (May 3) * Summer Purification Festival (June 30) * Autumn Grand Festival (Sacred Fire Festival) (September 15) * Zako Festival (second Sunday of October) * Ichiyō Raifuku Festival (winter solstice) === Appearances in works === * {{jalink|Matsuo Bashō|Q5676}} *Oi no Kobumi*{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}} *: {{Cquote|{{small|As I made a pilgrimage through the land of Yamato and passed the foot of Mount Katsuragi, flowers were in full bloom on all sides, the peaks veiled in haze—at dawn the scenery was all the more alluring. Hearing people speak ill of the appearance of that god,}}
Still I longed to see—through flowers, the dawning face of the god|20px||*Oi no Kobumi*}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Primary sources''' {{reflist|2|group="原"}} '''References''' {{reflist|2}} === {{wikidata link|Q11620885}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Katsuragi Hitokotonushi Shrine |Image = [[File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja haiden and icho.jpg|270px]]
Haiden and the lactiferous ginkgo
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |Location = Moriwaki 432, {{jalink|Nara Prefecture|Q131287}}{{jalink|Gose City|Q847334}} |ISO = JP-23 |Latitude degrees = 34|Latitude minutes = 26|Latitude seconds = 43.97 |Longitude degrees = 135|Longitude minutes = 42|Longitude seconds = 42.98 |Enshrined deities = {{jalink|Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|Katsuragi no Hitokotonushi Ōkami}}
{{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|Wakatakeru no Mikoto}} |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|Myōjin Great Shrine}})
Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Alternate name = |Pilgrimage site = |Annual festival = {{jalink|April 5|Q2507}}, {{jalink|September 15|Q2846}} |Rituals = |Map = Japan Nara }} '''Katsuragi Hitokotonushi Shrine''' (かつらぎひとことぬしじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in {{jalink|Gose City|Q847334}}, {{jalink|Nara Prefecture|Q131287}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and under the {{jalink|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|former shrine ranking system}} it was designated a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. It is commonly known as “Ichigon-san” or “Ichikon-ji-san.” The shrine is enshrined facing east on the eastern foothills of {{jalink|Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Q11433560|Mount Katsuragi}}. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following two deitiesShrine history pamphlet.. * {{jalink|Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|Katsuragi no Hitokotonushi Ōkami}} (かつらぎのひとことぬしのおおかみ) – principal deity. * {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|Wakatakeru no Mikoto}} (わかたけるのみこと) – the 21st Emperor Yūryaku. The Meiji-era *Shrine Register* lists the two deities as Kotoshironushi no Mikoto and Wakatakeru no Mikoto, while the *Yamato Shiryō* states that Hitokotonushi is the main deity with Emperor Yūryaku enshrined jointly{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}{{Sfn|奈良県史 5|1989年|pp=449-450}}. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* {{jalink|Register of Deities|Q11064932|Jinmyōchō}}, the shrine has one seat, and appears as “Katsuki-ni-masu Hitokotonushi Shrine,” indicating that it originally enshrined only {{jalink|Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|Hitokotonushi}}{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. This deity is written as “Hitokotonushi” in the *{{jalink|Kojiki|Q813031}}*, “Hito-kotonushi” in the *{{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}*, and “Hito-go-nushi” in the *{{jalink|Nihon Ryōiki|Q1606838|Nihon Ryōiki}}*. He is regarded as a god of {{jalink|oracles}}, who utters both good and ill fortune in a single word, and is still worshiped today as a deity who grants wishes with a single utterance{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. Although no genealogy is recorded in the *Kojiki* or *Nihon Shoki*, the *{{jalink|Sendai Kuji Hongi|Q2716751}}*“Sendai Kuji Hongi,” *Chigi Honki*. identifies Hitokotonushi as a child of {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. [[File:Tennō Yūryaku.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{Center|{{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} and the great boar of {{jalink|Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Q11433560|Mount Katsuragi}}}}]] Regarding Hitokotonushi, a well-known encounter tale with {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} appears in both the *Nihon Shoki**Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Yūryaku, 4th year, 2nd month. and the *Kojiki**Kojiki*, section on Emperor Yūryaku.. According to both accounts, while Emperor Yūryaku was hunting in the mountains of Katsuragi, Hitokotonushi (also written as Hito-kotonushi), who appeared in the same form as the emperor, emerged and competed with him in the hunt{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. In the *Kojiki*, however, the emperor offers a great sword, bow and arrows, and many garments to the deity and worships him, portraying Hitokotonushi as superior, whereas in the *Nihon Shoki* the emperor makes no such offerings and the two are depicted as nearly equal in status{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. Since the *Kojiki* is considered more archaic, its account is thought to reflect the political circumstances when the {{jalink|Katsuragi clan|Q11620908}}—regarded as the clan responsible for the worship of Hitokotonushi—held strong power as imperial in-laws, whereas the *Nihon Shoki* reflects a later period when the Katsuragi clan declined and the status of Hitokotonushi diminished{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}*Shinpen Nihon Koten Bungaku Zenshū 3: Nihon Shoki (2)*, Shogakukan, 2004 (JapanKnowledge edition), pp. 158–160.. In later periods, in Heian-era works such as the *{{jalink|Nihon Ryōiki|Q1606838|Nihon Ryōiki}}**Nihon Ryōiki*, volume 28. and the *{{jalink|Konjaku Monogatari-shū|Q1507085}}**Konjaku Monogatari-shū*, volume 11, story 3., Hitokotonushi is described as being conscripted by {{jalink|En no Gyōja}} (En no Ubasoku / En no Ozunu) to build a bridge between {{jalink|Mount Kinpu|Q10563986}} and Mount Katsuragi, and even bound by a curse after incurring En no Gyōja’s anger—indicating a further decline in divine authority{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. It is also said that during this forced labor, Hitokotonushi hid the ugliness of his face and worked only at night, not by day; inspired by this tale, {{jalink|Matsuo Bashō|Q5676}} composed a poem in *Oi no Kobumi* (see “{{jalink|#Appearances in works||Appearances in works}}”){{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. [[File:Tosa-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{jalink|Tosa Shrine|Q704890}} ({{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}{{jalink|Kōchi City|Q200251}})}}{{small|Currently enshrines {{jalink|Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|Hitokotonushi}} and {{jalink|Ajisukitakahikone||Ajisukitakahikone no Kami}}.}}]] According to the *{{jalink|Shoku Nihongi|Q611399}}*, entry for the 8th year of {{jalink|Tenpyō-hōji|Q1346307}} ({{jalink|764|Q31163}})*Shoku Nihongi*, Tenpyō-hōji 8 (764), 11th month, day 7., a deity called “Takakamo no Kami,” who had competed in hunting with Emperor Yūryaku, had been exiled to {{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330}}, but in that year was re-enshrined in his original place{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. However, one passage cited in the *{{jalink|Shaku Nihongi|Q2710483}}* from the *Rekiryoku* states that in Tenpyō-hōji 8, Hitokotonushi was brought back from Tosa and enshrined in Katsuragi{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. Another excerpt from the *Tosa Province Fudoki* cited in the *Shaku Nihongi* notes that the deity of Takakamo Taisha in Tosa (present-day {{jalink|Tosa Shrine|Q704890}}) is Hitokotonushi no Mikoto, though another tradition identifies him as {{jalink|Ajisukitakahikone|Q506610|Ajisukitakahikone no Mikoto}}. Thus, the sources show confusion between Hitokotonushi and Takakamo no Kami (Ajisukitakahikone no Mikoto, enshrined at {{jalink|Takakamo Shrine|Q11673295}}){{Sfn|平林章仁|2013年|pp=235-253}}. Additionally, due to similarities in name and their nature as oracle deities, some theories identify Hitokotonushi with {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi no Mikoto}}{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. === History === The date of foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition holds that the current site is where Hitokotonushi manifested. In the {{jalink|Six National Histories|Q1151168|official histories}}, it is recorded that in the 3rd year of {{jalink|Kashō|Q1373157}} ({{jalink|850|Q30364}}), the divine rank of “Katsuki Hitokotonushi no Kami” was raised to Senior Third Rank, and that in the 3rd year of {{jalink|Ten’an (Japanese era)|Q1075611|Ten’an}} ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) in the first month, it was promoted to Junior Second Rank, Second Class of Merit{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. In September of the same year, the first year of {{jalink|Jōgan (Japanese era)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} (859)*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, Jōgan 1 (859), 9th month, 8th day (see {{Harvnb|神道・神社史料集成}})., envoys were dispatched to present offerings to Hitokotonushi and other deities in prayer for rain and wind{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. In the 5th year of {{jalink|Shōryaku|Q1324858}} ({{jalink|994|Q24114}}), fourth month*Honchō Seiki*, Shōryaku 5 (994), 4th month., offerings were again presented, with members of the {{jalink|Nakatomi clan|Q1198447}} serving as envoys, in response to epidemics and fires{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* {{jalink|Register of Deities|Q11064932|Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274}} ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), the shrine is listed in Yamato Province, {{jalink|Katsukami District|Q11620858}}, as “Katsuki-ni-masu Hitokotonushi Shrine, Myōjin Taisha, Monthly and New Harvest Offerings,” indicating its status as a {{jalink|Myōjin Taisha}} and that it received offerings from the court at the {{jalink|Monthly Rite|Q11516161}} and {{jalink|New Harvest Festival|Q11501518}}{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. Subsequent history is unclear. In the past, there was a temple called Ichigon-ji to the southeast of the shrine as a {{jalink|jingū-ji|Q1074971}}, but it has since been abandoned{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6), the shrine was ranked as a {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}} under the {{jalink|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534}}, and in March {{jalink|1883|Q7816}} (Meiji 16) it was promoted to {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}{{Sfn|明治神社誌料|1912年}}. === Divine ranks === * {{jalink|Kashō|Q1373157}} 3 ({{jalink|850|Q30364}}), October 7: Senior Third Rank (*{{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}*)*Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku*, Kashō 3 (850), 10th month, day 7 (see {{Harvnb|神道・神社史料集成}}). – recorded as “Katsuki Hitokotonushi no Kami.” * {{jalink|Ten’an (Japanese era)|Q1075611|Ten’an}} 3 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}), January 27: Promoted from Senior Third Rank, Second Class of Merit to Junior Second Rank, Second Class of Merit (*{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}*)*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, Jōgan 1 (859), 1st month, 27th day (see {{Harvnb|神道・神社史料集成}}). – recorded as “Katsuki Hitokotonushi no Kami.” === Shrine grounds === [[File:Katusragi-hitokotonushi-jinja icho.jpg|thumb|180px|{{center|Lactiferous ginkgo}}]] * Main hall – Rebuilt in {{jalink|1876|Q7766}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 9). One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} style, with a copper-sheet roof{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. * Worship hall – In front of the hall stands an ancient {{jalink|ginkgo|Q43284}} tree (the lactiferous ginkgo), said to be 1,200 years old and regarded as a sacred tree. * Offering hall * Shrine office * Spider Mound – A mound based on legends of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}’s subjugation of the {{jalink|earth spiders|Q2063089}}{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. * Great Rite Memorial Hall * Statue of {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} * Hōrai Stone * Turtle Stone === Auxiliary and branch shrines === * Jingū Kōgō Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} * {{jalink|Tenman Shrine}} – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005}} * {{jalink|Hachiman Shrine}} – Enshrined deities: {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|Hondawake no Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803|Okinaga Tarashihime no Mikoto}} * Ichikishima Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|Ichikishimahime no Mikoto}} * Hitokoto {{jalink|Inari Shrine|Q514480}} – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ukemochi|Q2280323}} * Haraedo Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Seoritsuhime|Q13579836}} no Mikoto * {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Shrine|Q11381857}} – Enshrined deities: {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Sokotsutsuo no Mikoto, Nakatsutsuo no Mikoto, Uwatsutsuo no Mikoto}}, Okinaga Tarashihime no Mikoto * Okumiya – About 50 meters west of the main hall, a {{jalink|iwakura}} is enshrined as the inner shrine. File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, Kame Stone 01.jpg|Turtle Stone File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, Kumo Stone 01.jpg|Spider Mound File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, 07.jpg|First torii gate Katusragi-hitokotonushi-jinja keidaisha-2.jpg|Shrines within the grounds Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, Auxiliary Itokoto-Inari Shrine 01.jpg|Hitokoto Inari Shrine === Festivals === List of annual festivals held at Katsuragi Hitokotonushi Shrine. * New Year’s Day Festival (January 1) * Setsubun Festival (around February 3) * Spring Grand Festival (April 5) * Inari Shrine Festival (May 3) * Summer Purification Festival (June 30) * Autumn Grand Festival (Sacred Fire Festival) (September 15) * Zako Festival (second Sunday of October) * Ichiyō Raifuku Festival (winter solstice) === Appearances in works === * {{jalink|Matsuo Bashō|Q5676}} *Oi no Kobumi*{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}} *: {{Cquote|{{small|As I made a pilgrimage through the land of Yamato and passed the foot of Mount Katsuragi, flowers were in full bloom on all sides, the peaks veiled in haze—at dawn the scenery was all the more alluring. Hearing people speak ill of the appearance of that god,}}
Still I longed to see—through flowers, the dawning face of the god|20px||*Oi no Kobumi*}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Primary sources''' {{reflist|2|group="原"}} '''References''' {{reflist|2}} === {{wikidata link|Q11620885}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Katsuragi Hitokotonushi Shrine |Image = [[File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja haiden and icho.jpg|270px]]
Haiden and the lactiferous ginkgo
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |Location = Moriwaki 432, {{jalink|Nara Prefecture|Q131287}}{{jalink|Gose City|Q847334}} |ISO = JP-23 |Latitude degrees = 34|Latitude minutes = 26|Latitude seconds = 43.97 |Longitude degrees = 135|Longitude minutes = 42|Longitude seconds = 42.98 |Enshrined deities = {{jalink|Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|Katsuragi no Hitokotonushi Ōkami}}
{{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|Wakatakeru no Mikoto}} |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|Myōjin Great Shrine}})
Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Alternate name = |Pilgrimage site = |Annual festival = {{jalink|April 5|Q2507}}, {{jalink|September 15|Q2846}} |Rituals = |Map = Japan Nara }} '''Katsuragi Hitokotonushi Shrine''' (かつらぎひとことぬしじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in {{jalink|Gose City|Q847334}}, {{jalink|Nara Prefecture|Q131287}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and under the {{jalink|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|former shrine ranking system}} it was designated a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. It is commonly known as “Ichigon-san” or “Ichikon-ji-san.” The shrine is enshrined facing east on the eastern foothills of {{jalink|Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Q11433560|Mount Katsuragi}}. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following two deitiesShrine history pamphlet.. * {{jalink|Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|Katsuragi no Hitokotonushi Ōkami}} (かつらぎのひとことぬしのおおかみ) – principal deity. * {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|Wakatakeru no Mikoto}} (わかたけるのみこと) – the 21st Emperor Yūryaku. The Meiji-era *Shrine Register* lists the two deities as Kotoshironushi no Mikoto and Wakatakeru no Mikoto, while the *Yamato Shiryō* states that Hitokotonushi is the main deity with Emperor Yūryaku enshrined jointly{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}{{Sfn|奈良県史 5|1989年|pp=449-450}}. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* {{jalink|Register of Deities|Q11064932|Jinmyōchō}}, the shrine has one seat, and appears as “Katsuki-ni-masu Hitokotonushi Shrine,” indicating that it originally enshrined only {{jalink|Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|Hitokotonushi}}{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. This deity is written as “Hitokotonushi” in the *{{jalink|Kojiki|Q813031}}*, “Hito-kotonushi” in the *{{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}*, and “Hito-go-nushi” in the *{{jalink|Nihon Ryōiki|Q1606838|Nihon Ryōiki}}*. He is regarded as a god of {{jalink|oracles}}, who utters both good and ill fortune in a single word, and is still worshiped today as a deity who grants wishes with a single utterance{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. Although no genealogy is recorded in the *Kojiki* or *Nihon Shoki*, the *{{jalink|Sendai Kuji Hongi|Q2716751}}*“Sendai Kuji Hongi,” *Chigi Honki*. identifies Hitokotonushi as a child of {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. [[File:Tennō Yūryaku.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{Center|{{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} and the great boar of {{jalink|Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Q11433560|Mount Katsuragi}}}}]] Regarding Hitokotonushi, a well-known encounter tale with {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} appears in both the *Nihon Shoki**Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Yūryaku, 4th year, 2nd month. and the *Kojiki**Kojiki*, section on Emperor Yūryaku.. According to both accounts, while Emperor Yūryaku was hunting in the mountains of Katsuragi, Hitokotonushi (also written as Hito-kotonushi), who appeared in the same form as the emperor, emerged and competed with him in the hunt{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. In the *Kojiki*, however, the emperor offers a great sword, bow and arrows, and many garments to the deity and worships him, portraying Hitokotonushi as superior, whereas in the *Nihon Shoki* the emperor makes no such offerings and the two are depicted as nearly equal in status{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. Since the *Kojiki* is considered more archaic, its account is thought to reflect the political circumstances when the {{jalink|Katsuragi clan|Q11620908}}—regarded as the clan responsible for the worship of Hitokotonushi—held strong power as imperial in-laws, whereas the *Nihon Shoki* reflects a later period when the Katsuragi clan declined and the status of Hitokotonushi diminished{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}*Shinpen Nihon Koten Bungaku Zenshū 3: Nihon Shoki (2)*, Shogakukan, 2004 (JapanKnowledge edition), pp. 158–160.. In later periods, in Heian-era works such as the *{{jalink|Nihon Ryōiki|Q1606838|Nihon Ryōiki}}**Nihon Ryōiki*, volume 28. and the *{{jalink|Konjaku Monogatari-shū|Q1507085}}**Konjaku Monogatari-shū*, volume 11, story 3., Hitokotonushi is described as being conscripted by {{jalink|En no Gyōja}} (En no Ubasoku / En no Ozunu) to build a bridge between {{jalink|Mount Kinpu|Q10563986}} and Mount Katsuragi, and even bound by a curse after incurring En no Gyōja’s anger—indicating a further decline in divine authority{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. It is also said that during this forced labor, Hitokotonushi hid the ugliness of his face and worked only at night, not by day; inspired by this tale, {{jalink|Matsuo Bashō|Q5676}} composed a poem in *Oi no Kobumi* (see “{{jalink|#Appearances in works||Appearances in works}}”){{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. [[File:Tosa-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{jalink|Tosa Shrine|Q704890}} ({{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}{{jalink|Kōchi City|Q200251}})}}{{small|Currently enshrines {{jalink|Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|Hitokotonushi}} and {{jalink|Ajisukitakahikone||Ajisukitakahikone no Kami}}.}}]] According to the *{{jalink|Shoku Nihongi|Q611399}}*, entry for the 8th year of {{jalink|Tenpyō-hōji|Q1346307}} ({{jalink|764|Q31163}})*Shoku Nihongi*, Tenpyō-hōji 8 (764), 11th month, day 7., a deity called “Takakamo no Kami,” who had competed in hunting with Emperor Yūryaku, had been exiled to {{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330}}, but in that year was re-enshrined in his original place{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. However, one passage cited in the *{{jalink|Shaku Nihongi|Q2710483}}* from the *Rekiryoku* states that in Tenpyō-hōji 8, Hitokotonushi was brought back from Tosa and enshrined in Katsuragi{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. Another excerpt from the *Tosa Province Fudoki* cited in the *Shaku Nihongi* notes that the deity of Takakamo Taisha in Tosa (present-day {{jalink|Tosa Shrine|Q704890}}) is Hitokotonushi no Mikoto, though another tradition identifies him as {{jalink|Ajisukitakahikone|Q506610|Ajisukitakahikone no Mikoto}}. Thus, the sources show confusion between Hitokotonushi and Takakamo no Kami (Ajisukitakahikone no Mikoto, enshrined at {{jalink|Takakamo Shrine|Q11673295}}){{Sfn|平林章仁|2013年|pp=235-253}}. Additionally, due to similarities in name and their nature as oracle deities, some theories identify Hitokotonushi with {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi no Mikoto}}{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. === History === The date of foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition holds that the current site is where Hitokotonushi manifested. In the {{jalink|Six National Histories|Q1151168|official histories}}, it is recorded that in the 3rd year of {{jalink|Kashō|Q1373157}} ({{jalink|850|Q30364}}), the divine rank of “Katsuki Hitokotonushi no Kami” was raised to Senior Third Rank, and that in the 3rd year of {{jalink|Ten’an (Japanese era)|Q1075611|Ten’an}} ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) in the first month, it was promoted to Junior Second Rank, Second Class of Merit{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. In September of the same year, the first year of {{jalink|Jōgan (Japanese era)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} (859)*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, Jōgan 1 (859), 9th month, 8th day (see {{Harvnb|神道・神社史料集成}})., envoys were dispatched to present offerings to Hitokotonushi and other deities in prayer for rain and wind{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. In the 5th year of {{jalink|Shōryaku|Q1324858}} ({{jalink|994|Q24114}}), fourth month*Honchō Seiki*, Shōryaku 5 (994), 4th month., offerings were again presented, with members of the {{jalink|Nakatomi clan|Q1198447}} serving as envoys, in response to epidemics and fires{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* {{jalink|Register of Deities|Q11064932|Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274}} ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), the shrine is listed in Yamato Province, {{jalink|Katsukami District|Q11620858}}, as “Katsuki-ni-masu Hitokotonushi Shrine, Myōjin Taisha, Monthly and New Harvest Offerings,” indicating its status as a {{jalink|Myōjin Taisha}} and that it received offerings from the court at the {{jalink|Monthly Rite|Q11516161}} and {{jalink|New Harvest Festival|Q11501518}}{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. Subsequent history is unclear. In the past, there was a temple called Ichigon-ji to the southeast of the shrine as a {{jalink|jingū-ji|Q1074971}}, but it has since been abandoned{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6), the shrine was ranked as a {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}} under the {{jalink|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534}}, and in March {{jalink|1883|Q7816}} (Meiji 16) it was promoted to {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}{{Sfn|明治神社誌料|1912年}}. === Divine ranks === * {{jalink|Kashō|Q1373157}} 3 ({{jalink|850|Q30364}}), October 7: Senior Third Rank (*{{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}*)*Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku*, Kashō 3 (850), 10th month, day 7 (see {{Harvnb|神道・神社史料集成}}). – recorded as “Katsuki Hitokotonushi no Kami.” * {{jalink|Ten’an (Japanese era)|Q1075611|Ten’an}} 3 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}), January 27: Promoted from Senior Third Rank, Second Class of Merit to Junior Second Rank, Second Class of Merit (*{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}*)*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, Jōgan 1 (859), 1st month, 27th day (see {{Harvnb|神道・神社史料集成}}). – recorded as “Katsuki Hitokotonushi no Kami.” === Shrine grounds === [[File:Katusragi-hitokotonushi-jinja icho.jpg|thumb|180px|{{center|Lactiferous ginkgo}}]] * Main hall – Rebuilt in {{jalink|1876|Q7766}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 9). One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} style, with a copper-sheet roof{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. * Worship hall – In front of the hall stands an ancient {{jalink|ginkgo|Q43284}} tree (the lactiferous ginkgo), said to be 1,200 years old and regarded as a sacred tree. * Offering hall * Shrine office * Spider Mound – A mound based on legends of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}’s subjugation of the {{jalink|earth spiders|Q2063089}}{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. * Great Rite Memorial Hall * Statue of {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} * Hōrai Stone * Turtle Stone === Auxiliary and branch shrines === * Jingū Kōgō Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} * {{jalink|Tenman Shrine}} – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005}} * {{jalink|Hachiman Shrine}} – Enshrined deities: {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|Hondawake no Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803|Okinaga Tarashihime no Mikoto}} * Ichikishima Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|Ichikishimahime no Mikoto}} * Hitokoto {{jalink|Inari Shrine|Q514480}} – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ukemochi|Q2280323}} * Haraedo Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Seoritsuhime|Q13579836}} no Mikoto * {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Shrine|Q11381857}} – Enshrined deities: {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Sokotsutsuo no Mikoto, Nakatsutsuo no Mikoto, Uwatsutsuo no Mikoto}}, Okinaga Tarashihime no Mikoto * Okumiya – About 50 meters west of the main hall, a {{jalink|iwakura}} is enshrined as the inner shrine. File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, Kame Stone 01.jpg|Turtle Stone File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, Kumo Stone 01.jpg|Spider Mound File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, 07.jpg|First torii gate Katusragi-hitokotonushi-jinja keidaisha-2.jpg|Shrines within the grounds Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, Auxiliary Itokoto-Inari Shrine 01.jpg|Hitokoto Inari Shrine === Festivals === List of annual festivals held at Katsuragi Hitokotonushi Shrine. * New Year’s Day Festival (January 1) * Setsubun Festival (around February 3) * Spring Grand Festival (April 5) * Inari Shrine Festival (May 3) * Summer Purification Festival (June 30) * Autumn Grand Festival (Sacred Fire Festival) (September 15) * Zako Festival (second Sunday of October) * Ichiyō Raifuku Festival (winter solstice) === Appearances in works === * {{jalink|Matsuo Bashō|Q5676}} *Oi no Kobumi*{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}} *: {{Cquote|{{small|As I made a pilgrimage through the land of Yamato and passed the foot of Mount Katsuragi, flowers were in full bloom on all sides, the peaks veiled in haze—at dawn the scenery was all the more alluring. Hearing people speak ill of the appearance of that god,}}
Still I longed to see—through flowers, the dawning face of the god|20px||*Oi no Kobumi*}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Primary sources''' {{reflist|2|group="原"}} '''References''' {{reflist|2}} === {{wikidata link|Q11620885}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Katsuragi Hitokotonushi Shrine |Image = [[File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja haiden and icho.jpg|270px]]
Haiden and the lactiferous ginkgo
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |Location = Moriwaki 432, {{jalink|Nara Prefecture|Q131287}}{{jalink|Gose City|Q847334}} |ISO = JP-23 |Latitude degrees = 34|Latitude minutes = 26|Latitude seconds = 43.97 |Longitude degrees = 135|Longitude minutes = 42|Longitude seconds = 42.98 |Enshrined deities = {{jalink|Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|Katsuragi no Hitokotonushi Ōkami}}
{{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|Wakatakeru no Mikoto}} |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|Myōjin Great Shrine}})
Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Alternate name = |Pilgrimage site = |Annual festival = {{jalink|April 5|Q2507}}, {{jalink|September 15|Q2846}} |Rituals = |Map = Japan Nara }} '''Katsuragi Hitokotonushi Shrine''' (かつらぎひとことぬしじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in {{jalink|Gose City|Q847334}}, {{jalink|Nara Prefecture|Q131287}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and under the {{jalink|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|former shrine ranking system}} it was designated a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. It is commonly known as “Ichigon-san” or “Ichikon-ji-san.” The shrine is enshrined facing east on the eastern foothills of {{jalink|Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Q11433560|Mount Katsuragi}}. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following two deitiesShrine history pamphlet.. * {{jalink|Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|Katsuragi no Hitokotonushi Ōkami}} (かつらぎのひとことぬしのおおかみ) – principal deity. * {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|Wakatakeru no Mikoto}} (わかたけるのみこと) – the 21st Emperor Yūryaku. The Meiji-era *Shrine Register* lists the two deities as Kotoshironushi no Mikoto and Wakatakeru no Mikoto, while the *Yamato Shiryō* states that Hitokotonushi is the main deity with Emperor Yūryaku enshrined jointly{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}{{Sfn|奈良県史 5|1989年|pp=449-450}}. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* {{jalink|Register of Deities|Q11064932|Jinmyōchō}}, the shrine has one seat, and appears as “Katsuki-ni-masu Hitokotonushi Shrine,” indicating that it originally enshrined only {{jalink|Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|Hitokotonushi}}{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. This deity is written as “Hitokotonushi” in the *{{jalink|Kojiki|Q813031}}*, “Hito-kotonushi” in the *{{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}*, and “Hito-go-nushi” in the *{{jalink|Nihon Ryōiki|Q1606838|Nihon Ryōiki}}*. He is regarded as a god of {{jalink|oracles}}, who utters both good and ill fortune in a single word, and is still worshiped today as a deity who grants wishes with a single utterance{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. Although no genealogy is recorded in the *Kojiki* or *Nihon Shoki*, the *{{jalink|Sendai Kuji Hongi|Q2716751}}*“Sendai Kuji Hongi,” *Chigi Honki*. identifies Hitokotonushi as a child of {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. [[File:Tennō Yūryaku.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{Center|{{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} and the great boar of {{jalink|Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Q11433560|Mount Katsuragi}}}}]] Regarding Hitokotonushi, a well-known encounter tale with {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} appears in both the *Nihon Shoki**Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Yūryaku, 4th year, 2nd month. and the *Kojiki**Kojiki*, section on Emperor Yūryaku.. According to both accounts, while Emperor Yūryaku was hunting in the mountains of Katsuragi, Hitokotonushi (also written as Hito-kotonushi), who appeared in the same form as the emperor, emerged and competed with him in the hunt{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. In the *Kojiki*, however, the emperor offers a great sword, bow and arrows, and many garments to the deity and worships him, portraying Hitokotonushi as superior, whereas in the *Nihon Shoki* the emperor makes no such offerings and the two are depicted as nearly equal in status{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. Since the *Kojiki* is considered more archaic, its account is thought to reflect the political circumstances when the {{jalink|Katsuragi clan|Q11620908}}—regarded as the clan responsible for the worship of Hitokotonushi—held strong power as imperial in-laws, whereas the *Nihon Shoki* reflects a later period when the Katsuragi clan declined and the status of Hitokotonushi diminished{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}*Shinpen Nihon Koten Bungaku Zenshū 3: Nihon Shoki (2)*, Shogakukan, 2004 (JapanKnowledge edition), pp. 158–160.. In later periods, in Heian-era works such as the *{{jalink|Nihon Ryōiki|Q1606838|Nihon Ryōiki}}**Nihon Ryōiki*, volume 28. and the *{{jalink|Konjaku Monogatari-shū|Q1507085}}**Konjaku Monogatari-shū*, volume 11, story 3., Hitokotonushi is described as being conscripted by {{jalink|En no Gyōja}} (En no Ubasoku / En no Ozunu) to build a bridge between {{jalink|Mount Kinpu|Q10563986}} and Mount Katsuragi, and even bound by a curse after incurring En no Gyōja’s anger—indicating a further decline in divine authority{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. It is also said that during this forced labor, Hitokotonushi hid the ugliness of his face and worked only at night, not by day; inspired by this tale, {{jalink|Matsuo Bashō|Q5676}} composed a poem in *Oi no Kobumi* (see “{{jalink|#Appearances in works||Appearances in works}}”){{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. [[File:Tosa-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{jalink|Tosa Shrine|Q704890}} ({{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}{{jalink|Kōchi City|Q200251}})}}{{small|Currently enshrines {{jalink|Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|Hitokotonushi}} and {{jalink|Ajisukitakahikone||Ajisukitakahikone no Kami}}.}}]] According to the *{{jalink|Shoku Nihongi|Q611399}}*, entry for the 8th year of {{jalink|Tenpyō-hōji|Q1346307}} ({{jalink|764|Q31163}})*Shoku Nihongi*, Tenpyō-hōji 8 (764), 11th month, day 7., a deity called “Takakamo no Kami,” who had competed in hunting with Emperor Yūryaku, had been exiled to {{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330}}, but in that year was re-enshrined in his original place{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. However, one passage cited in the *{{jalink|Shaku Nihongi|Q2710483}}* from the *Rekiryoku* states that in Tenpyō-hōji 8, Hitokotonushi was brought back from Tosa and enshrined in Katsuragi{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. Another excerpt from the *Tosa Province Fudoki* cited in the *Shaku Nihongi* notes that the deity of Takakamo Taisha in Tosa (present-day {{jalink|Tosa Shrine|Q704890}}) is Hitokotonushi no Mikoto, though another tradition identifies him as {{jalink|Ajisukitakahikone|Q506610|Ajisukitakahikone no Mikoto}}. Thus, the sources show confusion between Hitokotonushi and Takakamo no Kami (Ajisukitakahikone no Mikoto, enshrined at {{jalink|Takakamo Shrine|Q11673295}}){{Sfn|平林章仁|2013年|pp=235-253}}. Additionally, due to similarities in name and their nature as oracle deities, some theories identify Hitokotonushi with {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi no Mikoto}}{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. === History === The date of foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition holds that the current site is where Hitokotonushi manifested. In the {{jalink|Six National Histories|Q1151168|official histories}}, it is recorded that in the 3rd year of {{jalink|Kashō|Q1373157}} ({{jalink|850|Q30364}}), the divine rank of “Katsuki Hitokotonushi no Kami” was raised to Senior Third Rank, and that in the 3rd year of {{jalink|Ten’an (Japanese era)|Q1075611|Ten’an}} ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) in the first month, it was promoted to Junior Second Rank, Second Class of Merit{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. In September of the same year, the first year of {{jalink|Jōgan (Japanese era)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} (859)*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, Jōgan 1 (859), 9th month, 8th day (see {{Harvnb|神道・神社史料集成}})., envoys were dispatched to present offerings to Hitokotonushi and other deities in prayer for rain and wind{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. In the 5th year of {{jalink|Shōryaku|Q1324858}} ({{jalink|994|Q24114}}), fourth month*Honchō Seiki*, Shōryaku 5 (994), 4th month., offerings were again presented, with members of the {{jalink|Nakatomi clan|Q1198447}} serving as envoys, in response to epidemics and fires{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* {{jalink|Register of Deities|Q11064932|Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274}} ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), the shrine is listed in Yamato Province, {{jalink|Katsukami District|Q11620858}}, as “Katsuki-ni-masu Hitokotonushi Shrine, Myōjin Taisha, Monthly and New Harvest Offerings,” indicating its status as a {{jalink|Myōjin Taisha}} and that it received offerings from the court at the {{jalink|Monthly Rite|Q11516161}} and {{jalink|New Harvest Festival|Q11501518}}{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. Subsequent history is unclear. In the past, there was a temple called Ichigon-ji to the southeast of the shrine as a {{jalink|jingū-ji|Q1074971}}, but it has since been abandoned{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6), the shrine was ranked as a {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}} under the {{jalink|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534}}, and in March {{jalink|1883|Q7816}} (Meiji 16) it was promoted to {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}{{Sfn|明治神社誌料|1912年}}. === Divine ranks === * {{jalink|Kashō|Q1373157}} 3 ({{jalink|850|Q30364}}), October 7: Senior Third Rank (*{{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}*)*Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku*, Kashō 3 (850), 10th month, day 7 (see {{Harvnb|神道・神社史料集成}}). – recorded as “Katsuki Hitokotonushi no Kami.” * {{jalink|Ten’an (Japanese era)|Q1075611|Ten’an}} 3 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}), January 27: Promoted from Senior Third Rank, Second Class of Merit to Junior Second Rank, Second Class of Merit (*{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}*)*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, Jōgan 1 (859), 1st month, 27th day (see {{Harvnb|神道・神社史料集成}}). – recorded as “Katsuki Hitokotonushi no Kami.” === Shrine grounds === [[File:Katusragi-hitokotonushi-jinja icho.jpg|thumb|180px|{{center|Lactiferous ginkgo}}]] * Main hall – Rebuilt in {{jalink|1876|Q7766}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 9). One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} style, with a copper-sheet roof{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. * Worship hall – In front of the hall stands an ancient {{jalink|ginkgo|Q43284}} tree (the lactiferous ginkgo), said to be 1,200 years old and regarded as a sacred tree. * Offering hall * Shrine office * Spider Mound – A mound based on legends of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}’s subjugation of the {{jalink|earth spiders|Q2063089}}{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. * Great Rite Memorial Hall * Statue of {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} * Hōrai Stone * Turtle Stone === Auxiliary and branch shrines === * Jingū Kōgō Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} * {{jalink|Tenman Shrine}} – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005}} * {{jalink|Hachiman Shrine}} – Enshrined deities: {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|Hondawake no Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803|Okinaga Tarashihime no Mikoto}} * Ichikishima Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|Ichikishimahime no Mikoto}} * Hitokoto {{jalink|Inari Shrine|Q514480}} – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ukemochi|Q2280323}} * Haraedo Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Seoritsuhime|Q13579836}} no Mikoto * {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Shrine|Q11381857}} – Enshrined deities: {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Sokotsutsuo no Mikoto, Nakatsutsuo no Mikoto, Uwatsutsuo no Mikoto}}, Okinaga Tarashihime no Mikoto * Okumiya – About 50 meters west of the main hall, a {{jalink|iwakura}} is enshrined as the inner shrine. File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, Kame Stone 01.jpg|Turtle Stone File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, Kumo Stone 01.jpg|Spider Mound File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, 07.jpg|First torii gate Katusragi-hitokotonushi-jinja keidaisha-2.jpg|Shrines within the grounds Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, Auxiliary Itokoto-Inari Shrine 01.jpg|Hitokoto Inari Shrine === Festivals === List of annual festivals held at Katsuragi Hitokotonushi Shrine. * New Year’s Day Festival (January 1) * Setsubun Festival (around February 3) * Spring Grand Festival (April 5) * Inari Shrine Festival (May 3) * Summer Purification Festival (June 30) * Autumn Grand Festival (Sacred Fire Festival) (September 15) * Zako Festival (second Sunday of October) * Ichiyō Raifuku Festival (winter solstice) === Appearances in works === * {{jalink|Matsuo Bashō|Q5676}} *Oi no Kobumi*{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}} *: {{Cquote|{{small|As I made a pilgrimage through the land of Yamato and passed the foot of Mount Katsuragi, flowers were in full bloom on all sides, the peaks veiled in haze—at dawn the scenery was all the more alluring. Hearing people speak ill of the appearance of that god,}}
Still I longed to see—through flowers, the dawning face of the god|20px||*Oi no Kobumi*}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Primary sources''' {{reflist|2|group="原"}} '''References''' {{reflist|2}} === Former {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Alternate name = |Pilgrimage site = |Annual festival = {{jalink|April 5|Q2507}}, {{jalink|September 15|Q2846}} |Rituals = |Map = Japan Nara }} '''Katsuragi Hitokotonushi Shrine''' (かつらぎひとことぬしじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} located in {{jalink|Gose City|Q847334}}, {{jalink|Nara Prefecture|Q131287}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and under the {{jalink|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|former shrine ranking system}} it was designated a {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}. It is commonly known as “Ichigon-san” or “Ichikon-ji-san.” The shrine is enshrined facing east on the eastern foothills of {{jalink|Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Q11433560|Mount Katsuragi}}. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following two deitiesShrine history pamphlet.. * {{jalink|Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|Katsuragi no Hitokotonushi Ōkami}} (かつらぎのひとことぬしのおおかみ) – principal deity. * {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|Wakatakeru no Mikoto}} (わかたけるのみこと) – the 21st Emperor Yūryaku. The Meiji-era *Shrine Register* lists the two deities as Kotoshironushi no Mikoto and Wakatakeru no Mikoto, while the *Yamato Shiryō* states that Hitokotonushi is the main deity with Emperor Yūryaku enshrined jointly{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}{{Sfn|奈良県史 5|1989年|pp=449-450}}. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* {{jalink|Register of Deities|Q11064932|Jinmyōchō}}, the shrine has one seat, and appears as “Katsuki-ni-masu Hitokotonushi Shrine,” indicating that it originally enshrined only {{jalink|Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|Hitokotonushi}}{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. This deity is written as “Hitokotonushi” in the *{{jalink|Kojiki|Q813031}}*, “Hito-kotonushi” in the *{{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}*, and “Hito-go-nushi” in the *{{jalink|Nihon Ryōiki|Q1606838|Nihon Ryōiki}}*. He is regarded as a god of {{jalink|oracles}}, who utters both good and ill fortune in a single word, and is still worshiped today as a deity who grants wishes with a single utterance{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. Although no genealogy is recorded in the *Kojiki* or *Nihon Shoki*, the *{{jalink|Sendai Kuji Hongi|Q2716751}}*“Sendai Kuji Hongi,” *Chigi Honki*. identifies Hitokotonushi as a child of {{jalink|Susanoo|Q272993|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. [[File:Tennō Yūryaku.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{Center|{{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} and the great boar of {{jalink|Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Q11433560|Mount Katsuragi}}}}]] Regarding Hitokotonushi, a well-known encounter tale with {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} appears in both the *Nihon Shoki**Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Yūryaku, 4th year, 2nd month. and the *Kojiki**Kojiki*, section on Emperor Yūryaku.. According to both accounts, while Emperor Yūryaku was hunting in the mountains of Katsuragi, Hitokotonushi (also written as Hito-kotonushi), who appeared in the same form as the emperor, emerged and competed with him in the hunt{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. In the *Kojiki*, however, the emperor offers a great sword, bow and arrows, and many garments to the deity and worships him, portraying Hitokotonushi as superior, whereas in the *Nihon Shoki* the emperor makes no such offerings and the two are depicted as nearly equal in status{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. Since the *Kojiki* is considered more archaic, its account is thought to reflect the political circumstances when the {{jalink|Katsuragi clan|Q11620908}}—regarded as the clan responsible for the worship of Hitokotonushi—held strong power as imperial in-laws, whereas the *Nihon Shoki* reflects a later period when the Katsuragi clan declined and the status of Hitokotonushi diminished{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}*Shinpen Nihon Koten Bungaku Zenshū 3: Nihon Shoki (2)*, Shogakukan, 2004 (JapanKnowledge edition), pp. 158–160.. In later periods, in Heian-era works such as the *{{jalink|Nihon Ryōiki|Q1606838|Nihon Ryōiki}}**Nihon Ryōiki*, volume 28. and the *{{jalink|Konjaku Monogatari-shū|Q1507085}}**Konjaku Monogatari-shū*, volume 11, story 3., Hitokotonushi is described as being conscripted by {{jalink|En no Gyōja}} (En no Ubasoku / En no Ozunu) to build a bridge between {{jalink|Mount Kinpu|Q10563986}} and Mount Katsuragi, and even bound by a curse after incurring En no Gyōja’s anger—indicating a further decline in divine authority{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. It is also said that during this forced labor, Hitokotonushi hid the ugliness of his face and worked only at night, not by day; inspired by this tale, {{jalink|Matsuo Bashō|Q5676}} composed a poem in *Oi no Kobumi* (see “{{jalink|#Appearances in works||Appearances in works}}”){{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. [[File:Tosa-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{jalink|Tosa Shrine|Q704890}} ({{jalink|Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093}}{{jalink|Kōchi City|Q200251}})}}{{small|Currently enshrines {{jalink|Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|Hitokotonushi}} and {{jalink|Ajisukitakahikone||Ajisukitakahikone no Kami}}.}}]] According to the *{{jalink|Shoku Nihongi|Q611399}}*, entry for the 8th year of {{jalink|Tenpyō-hōji|Q1346307}} ({{jalink|764|Q31163}})*Shoku Nihongi*, Tenpyō-hōji 8 (764), 11th month, day 7., a deity called “Takakamo no Kami,” who had competed in hunting with Emperor Yūryaku, had been exiled to {{jalink|Tosa Province|Q857330}}, but in that year was re-enshrined in his original place{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. However, one passage cited in the *{{jalink|Shaku Nihongi|Q2710483}}* from the *Rekiryoku* states that in Tenpyō-hōji 8, Hitokotonushi was brought back from Tosa and enshrined in Katsuragi{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. Another excerpt from the *Tosa Province Fudoki* cited in the *Shaku Nihongi* notes that the deity of Takakamo Taisha in Tosa (present-day {{jalink|Tosa Shrine|Q704890}}) is Hitokotonushi no Mikoto, though another tradition identifies him as {{jalink|Ajisukitakahikone|Q506610|Ajisukitakahikone no Mikoto}}. Thus, the sources show confusion between Hitokotonushi and Takakamo no Kami (Ajisukitakahikone no Mikoto, enshrined at {{jalink|Takakamo Shrine|Q11673295}}){{Sfn|平林章仁|2013年|pp=235-253}}. Additionally, due to similarities in name and their nature as oracle deities, some theories identify Hitokotonushi with {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi no Mikoto}}{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. === History === The date of foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition holds that the current site is where Hitokotonushi manifested. In the {{jalink|Six National Histories|Q1151168|official histories}}, it is recorded that in the 3rd year of {{jalink|Kashō|Q1373157}} ({{jalink|850|Q30364}}), the divine rank of “Katsuki Hitokotonushi no Kami” was raised to Senior Third Rank, and that in the 3rd year of {{jalink|Ten’an (Japanese era)|Q1075611|Ten’an}} ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}) in the first month, it was promoted to Junior Second Rank, Second Class of Merit{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. In September of the same year, the first year of {{jalink|Jōgan (Japanese era)|Q1075635|Jōgan}} (859)*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, Jōgan 1 (859), 9th month, 8th day (see {{Harvnb|神道・神社史料集成}})., envoys were dispatched to present offerings to Hitokotonushi and other deities in prayer for rain and wind{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. In the 5th year of {{jalink|Shōryaku|Q1324858}} ({{jalink|994|Q24114}}), fourth month*Honchō Seiki*, Shōryaku 5 (994), 4th month., offerings were again presented, with members of the {{jalink|Nakatomi clan|Q1198447}} serving as envoys, in response to epidemics and fires{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. In the *{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}* {{jalink|Register of Deities|Q11064932|Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274}} ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}), the shrine is listed in Yamato Province, {{jalink|Katsukami District|Q11620858}}, as “Katsuki-ni-masu Hitokotonushi Shrine, Myōjin Taisha, Monthly and New Harvest Offerings,” indicating its status as a {{jalink|Myōjin Taisha}} and that it received offerings from the court at the {{jalink|Monthly Rite|Q11516161}} and {{jalink|New Harvest Festival|Q11501518}}{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. Subsequent history is unclear. In the past, there was a temple called Ichigon-ji to the southeast of the shrine as a {{jalink|jingū-ji|Q1074971}}, but it has since been abandoned{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6), the shrine was ranked as a {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}} under the {{jalink|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534}}, and in March {{jalink|1883|Q7816}} (Meiji 16) it was promoted to {{jalink|Prefectural Shrine}}{{Sfn|明治神社誌料|1912年}}. === Divine ranks === * {{jalink|Kashō|Q1373157}} 3 ({{jalink|850|Q30364}}), October 7: Senior Third Rank (*{{jalink|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905}}*)*Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku*, Kashō 3 (850), 10th month, day 7 (see {{Harvnb|神道・神社史料集成}}). – recorded as “Katsuki Hitokotonushi no Kami.” * {{jalink|Ten’an (Japanese era)|Q1075611|Ten’an}} 3 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}), January 27: Promoted from Senior Third Rank, Second Class of Merit to Junior Second Rank, Second Class of Merit (*{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}*)*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, Jōgan 1 (859), 1st month, 27th day (see {{Harvnb|神道・神社史料集成}}). – recorded as “Katsuki Hitokotonushi no Kami.” === Shrine grounds === [[File:Katusragi-hitokotonushi-jinja icho.jpg|thumb|180px|{{center|Lactiferous ginkgo}}]] * Main hall – Rebuilt in {{jalink|1876|Q7766}} ({{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 9). One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} style, with a copper-sheet roof{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. * Worship hall – In front of the hall stands an ancient {{jalink|ginkgo|Q43284}} tree (the lactiferous ginkgo), said to be 1,200 years old and regarded as a sacred tree. * Offering hall * Shrine office * Spider Mound – A mound based on legends of {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}’s subjugation of the {{jalink|earth spiders|Q2063089}}{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. * Great Rite Memorial Hall * Statue of {{jalink|Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235}} * Hōrai Stone * Turtle Stone === Auxiliary and branch shrines === * Jingū Kōgō Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803}} * {{jalink|Tenman Shrine}} – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005}} * {{jalink|Hachiman Shrine}} – Enshrined deities: {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|Hondawake no Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Empress Jingū|Q232803|Okinaga Tarashihime no Mikoto}} * Ichikishima Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|Ichikishimahime no Mikoto}} * Hitokoto {{jalink|Inari Shrine|Q514480}} – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Ukemochi|Q2280323}} * Haraedo Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Seoritsuhime|Q13579836}} no Mikoto * {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Shrine|Q11381857}} – Enshrined deities: {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|Sokotsutsuo no Mikoto, Nakatsutsuo no Mikoto, Uwatsutsuo no Mikoto}}, Okinaga Tarashihime no Mikoto * Okumiya – About 50 meters west of the main hall, a {{jalink|iwakura}} is enshrined as the inner shrine. File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, Kame Stone 01.jpg|Turtle Stone File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, Kumo Stone 01.jpg|Spider Mound File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, 07.jpg|First torii gate Katusragi-hitokotonushi-jinja keidaisha-2.jpg|Shrines within the grounds Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, Auxiliary Itokoto-Inari Shrine 01.jpg|Hitokoto Inari Shrine === Festivals === List of annual festivals held at Katsuragi Hitokotonushi Shrine. * New Year’s Day Festival (January 1) * Setsubun Festival (around February 3) * Spring Grand Festival (April 5) * Inari Shrine Festival (May 3) * Summer Purification Festival (June 30) * Autumn Grand Festival (Sacred Fire Festival) (September 15) * Zako Festival (second Sunday of October) * Ichiyō Raifuku Festival (winter solstice) === Appearances in works === * {{jalink|Matsuo Bashō|Q5676}} *Oi no Kobumi*{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}} *: {{Cquote|{{small|As I made a pilgrimage through the land of Yamato and passed the foot of Mount Katsuragi, flowers were in full bloom on all sides, the peaks veiled in haze—at dawn the scenery was all the more alluring. Hearing people speak ill of the appearance of that god,}}
Still I longed to see—through flowers, the dawning face of the god|20px||*Oi no Kobumi*}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Primary sources''' {{reflist|2|group="原"}} '''References''' {{reflist|2}} === {{wikidata link|Q11620885}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Katsuragi Hitokotonushi Shrine |Image = [[File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja haiden and icho.jpg|270px]]
Haiden and the lactiferous ginkgo
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |Location = Moriwaki 432, {{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|WD=Q131287}}{{ill|lt=Gose City|Q847334|WD=Q847334}} |ISO = JP-23 |Latitude degrees = 34|Latitude minutes = 26|Latitude seconds = 43.97 |Longitude degrees = 135|Longitude minutes = 42|Longitude seconds = 42.98 |Enshrined deities = {{ill|lt=Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|WD=Q10864079|lt=Katsuragi no Hitokotonushi Ōkami}}
{{ill|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|WD=Q357235|lt=Wakatakeru no Mikoto}} |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Great Shrine}})
Former {{ill||lt=Prefectural Shrine|UNKNOWN}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = One-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Alternate name = |Pilgrimage site = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=April 5|Q2507|WD=Q2507}}, {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846}} |Rituals = |Map = Japan Nara }} '''Katsuragi Hitokotonushi Shrine''' (かつらぎひとことぬしじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Gose City|Q847334|WD=Q847334}}, {{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|WD=Q131287}}. It is a {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}), and under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system}} it was designated a {{ill||lt=Prefectural Shrine|UNKNOWN}}. It is commonly known as “Ichigon-san” or “Ichikon-ji-san.” The shrine is enshrined facing east on the eastern foothills of {{ill|lt=Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Q11433560|WD=Q11433560|lt=Mount Katsuragi}}. === Enshrined deities === The shrine enshrines the following two deitiesShrine history pamphlet.. * {{ill|lt=Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|WD=Q10864079|lt=Katsuragi no Hitokotonushi Ōkami}} (かつらぎのひとことぬしのおおかみ) – principal deity. * {{ill|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|WD=Q357235|lt=Wakatakeru no Mikoto}} (わかたけるのみこと) – the 21st Emperor Yūryaku. The Meiji-era *Shrine Register* lists the two deities as Kotoshironushi no Mikoto and Wakatakeru no Mikoto, while the *Yamato Shiryō* states that Hitokotonushi is the main deity with Emperor Yūryaku enshrined jointly{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}{{Sfn|奈良県史 5|1989年|pp=449-450}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}* {{ill|lt=Register of Deities|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Jinmyōchō}}, the shrine has one seat, and appears as “Katsuki-ni-masu Hitokotonushi Shrine,” indicating that it originally enshrined only {{ill|lt=Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|WD=Q10864079|lt=Hitokotonushi}}{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. This deity is written as “Hitokotonushi” in the *{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031}}*, “Hito-kotonushi” in the *{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}}*, and “Hito-go-nushi” in the *{{ill|lt=Nihon Ryōiki|Q1606838|WD=Q1606838|lt=Nihon Ryōiki}}*. He is regarded as a god of {{ill||lt=oracles|UNKNOWN}}, who utters both good and ill fortune in a single word, and is still worshiped today as a deity who grants wishes with a single utterance{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. Although no genealogy is recorded in the *Kojiki* or *Nihon Shoki*, the *{{ill|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi|Q2716751|WD=Q2716751}}*“Sendai Kuji Hongi,” *Chigi Honki*. identifies Hitokotonushi as a child of {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. [[File:Tennō Yūryaku.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{Center|{{ill|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|WD=Q357235}} and the great boar of {{ill|lt=Mount Yamato Katsuragi|Q11433560|WD=Q11433560|lt=Mount Katsuragi}}}}]] Regarding Hitokotonushi, a well-known encounter tale with {{ill|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|WD=Q357235}} appears in both the *Nihon Shoki**Nihon Shoki*, Emperor Yūryaku, 4th year, 2nd month. and the *Kojiki**Kojiki*, section on Emperor Yūryaku.. According to both accounts, while Emperor Yūryaku was hunting in the mountains of Katsuragi, Hitokotonushi (also written as Hito-kotonushi), who appeared in the same form as the emperor, emerged and competed with him in the hunt{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. In the *Kojiki*, however, the emperor offers a great sword, bow and arrows, and many garments to the deity and worships him, portraying Hitokotonushi as superior, whereas in the *Nihon Shoki* the emperor makes no such offerings and the two are depicted as nearly equal in status{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. Since the *Kojiki* is considered more archaic, its account is thought to reflect the political circumstances when the {{ill|lt=Katsuragi clan|Q11620908|WD=Q11620908}}—regarded as the clan responsible for the worship of Hitokotonushi—held strong power as imperial in-laws, whereas the *Nihon Shoki* reflects a later period when the Katsuragi clan declined and the status of Hitokotonushi diminished{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}*Shinpen Nihon Koten Bungaku Zenshū 3: Nihon Shoki (2)*, Shogakukan, 2004 (JapanKnowledge edition), pp. 158–160.. In later periods, in Heian-era works such as the *{{ill|lt=Nihon Ryōiki|Q1606838|WD=Q1606838|lt=Nihon Ryōiki}}**Nihon Ryōiki*, volume 28. and the *{{ill|lt=Konjaku Monogatari-shū|Q1507085|WD=Q1507085}}**Konjaku Monogatari-shū*, volume 11, story 3., Hitokotonushi is described as being conscripted by {{ill||lt=En no Gyōja|UNKNOWN}} (En no Ubasoku / En no Ozunu) to build a bridge between {{ill|lt=Mount Kinpu|Q10563986|WD=Q10563986}} and Mount Katsuragi, and even bound by a curse after incurring En no Gyōja’s anger—indicating a further decline in divine authority{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. It is also said that during this forced labor, Hitokotonushi hid the ugliness of his face and worked only at night, not by day; inspired by this tale, {{ill|lt=Matsuo Bashō|Q5676|WD=Q5676}} composed a poem in *Oi no Kobumi* (see “{{ill|lt=#Appearances in works||WD=|lt=Appearances in works}}”){{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. [[File:Tosa-jinja torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|{{ill|lt=Tosa Shrine|Q704890|WD=Q704890}} ({{ill|lt=Kōchi Prefecture|Q134093|WD=Q134093}}{{ill|lt=Kōchi City|Q200251|WD=Q200251}})}}{{small|Currently enshrines {{ill|lt=Hitokotonushi|Q10864079|WD=Q10864079|lt=Hitokotonushi}} and {{ill|lt=Ajisukitakahikone||WD=|lt=Ajisukitakahikone no Kami}}.}}]] According to the *{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|WD=Q611399}}*, entry for the 8th year of {{ill|lt=Tenpyō-hōji|Q1346307|WD=Q1346307}} ({{ill|lt=764|Q31163|WD=Q31163}})*Shoku Nihongi*, Tenpyō-hōji 8 (764), 11th month, day 7., a deity called “Takakamo no Kami,” who had competed in hunting with Emperor Yūryaku, had been exiled to {{ill|lt=Tosa Province|Q857330|WD=Q857330}}, but in that year was re-enshrined in his original place{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. However, one passage cited in the *{{ill|lt=Shaku Nihongi|Q2710483|WD=Q2710483}}* from the *Rekiryoku* states that in Tenpyō-hōji 8, Hitokotonushi was brought back from Tosa and enshrined in Katsuragi{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. Another excerpt from the *Tosa Province Fudoki* cited in the *Shaku Nihongi* notes that the deity of Takakamo Taisha in Tosa (present-day {{ill|lt=Tosa Shrine|Q704890|WD=Q704890}}) is Hitokotonushi no Mikoto, though another tradition identifies him as {{ill|lt=Ajisukitakahikone|Q506610|WD=Q506610|lt=Ajisukitakahikone no Mikoto}}. Thus, the sources show confusion between Hitokotonushi and Takakamo no Kami (Ajisukitakahikone no Mikoto, enshrined at {{ill|lt=Takakamo Shrine|Q11673295|WD=Q11673295}}){{Sfn|平林章仁|2013年|pp=235-253}}. Additionally, due to similarities in name and their nature as oracle deities, some theories identify Hitokotonushi with {{ill|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi no Mikoto}}{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. === History === The date of foundation is unknown. Shrine tradition holds that the current site is where Hitokotonushi manifested. In the {{ill|lt=Six National Histories|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|lt=official histories}}, it is recorded that in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Kashō|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157}} ({{ill|lt=850|Q30364|WD=Q30364}}), the divine rank of “Katsuki Hitokotonushi no Kami” was raised to Senior Third Rank, and that in the 3rd year of {{ill|lt=Ten’an (Japanese era)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten’an}} ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}) in the first month, it was promoted to Junior Second Rank, Second Class of Merit{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. In September of the same year, the first year of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japanese era)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} (859)*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, Jōgan 1 (859), 9th month, 8th day (see {{Harvnb|神道・神社史料集成}})., envoys were dispatched to present offerings to Hitokotonushi and other deities in prayer for rain and wind{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. In the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Shōryaku|Q1324858|WD=Q1324858}} ({{ill|lt=994|Q24114|WD=Q24114}}), fourth month*Honchō Seiki*, Shōryaku 5 (994), 4th month., offerings were again presented, with members of the {{ill|lt=Nakatomi clan|Q1198447|WD=Q1198447}} serving as envoys, in response to epidemics and fires{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. In the *{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}* {{ill|lt=Register of Deities|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Jinmyōchō}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274}} ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}), the shrine is listed in Yamato Province, {{ill|lt=Katsukami District|Q11620858|WD=Q11620858}}, as “Katsuki-ni-masu Hitokotonushi Shrine, Myōjin Taisha, Monthly and New Harvest Offerings,” indicating its status as a {{ill||lt=Myōjin Taisha|UNKNOWN}} and that it received offerings from the court at the {{ill|lt=Monthly Rite|Q11516161|WD=Q11516161}} and {{ill|lt=New Harvest Festival|Q11501518|WD=Q11501518}}{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. Subsequent history is unclear. In the past, there was a temple called Ichigon-ji to the southeast of the shrine as a {{ill|lt=jingū-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}, but it has since been abandoned{{Sfn|一言主神社（平凡社）|1981年}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, in {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6), the shrine was ranked as a {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} under the {{ill|lt=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}, and in March {{ill|lt=1883|Q7816|WD=Q7816}} (Meiji 16) it was promoted to {{ill||lt=Prefectural Shrine|UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn|明治神社誌料|1912年}}. === Divine ranks === * {{ill|lt=Kashō|Q1373157|WD=Q1373157}} 3 ({{ill|lt=850|Q30364|WD=Q30364}}), October 7: Senior Third Rank (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|Q1052905|WD=Q1052905}}*)*Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku*, Kashō 3 (850), 10th month, day 7 (see {{Harvnb|神道・神社史料集成}}). – recorded as “Katsuki Hitokotonushi no Kami.” * {{ill|lt=Ten’an (Japanese era)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten’an}} 3 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}), January 27: Promoted from Senior Third Rank, Second Class of Merit to Junior Second Rank, Second Class of Merit (*{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}*)*Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku*, Jōgan 1 (859), 1st month, 27th day (see {{Harvnb|神道・神社史料集成}}). – recorded as “Katsuki Hitokotonushi no Kami.” === Shrine grounds === [[File:Katusragi-hitokotonushi-jinja icho.jpg|thumb|180px|{{center|Lactiferous ginkgo}}]] * Main hall – Rebuilt in {{ill|lt=1876|Q7766|WD=Q7766}} ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 9). One-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} style, with a copper-sheet roof{{Sfn|葛城坐一言主神社（式内社）|1982年}}. * Worship hall – In front of the hall stands an ancient {{ill|lt=ginkgo|Q43284|WD=Q43284}} tree (the lactiferous ginkgo), said to be 1,200 years old and regarded as a sacred tree. * Offering hall * Shrine office * Spider Mound – A mound based on legends of {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188}}’s subjugation of the {{ill|lt=earth spiders|Q2063089|WD=Q2063089}}{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}}. * Great Rite Memorial Hall * Statue of {{ill|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|Q357235|WD=Q357235}} * Hōrai Stone * Turtle Stone === Auxiliary and branch shrines === * Jingū Kōgō Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803}} * {{ill||lt=Tenman Shrine|UNKNOWN}} – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005}} * {{ill||lt=Hachiman Shrine|UNKNOWN}} – Enshrined deities: {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake no Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Empress Jingū|Q232803|WD=Q232803|lt=Okinaga Tarashihime no Mikoto}} * Ichikishima Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ichikishimahime|Q11059454|WD=Q11059454|lt=Ichikishimahime no Mikoto}} * Hitokoto {{ill|lt=Inari Shrine|Q514480|WD=Q514480}} – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ukemochi|Q2280323|WD=Q2280323}} * Haraedo Shrine – Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Seoritsuhime|Q13579836|WD=Q13579836}} no Mikoto * {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine|Q11381857|WD=Q11381857}} – Enshrined deities: {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Sanjin|Q1114013|WD=Q1114013|lt=Sokotsutsuo no Mikoto, Nakatsutsuo no Mikoto, Uwatsutsuo no Mikoto}}, Okinaga Tarashihime no Mikoto * Okumiya – About 50 meters west of the main hall, a {{ill||lt=iwakura|UNKNOWN}} is enshrined as the inner shrine. File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, Kame Stone 01.jpg|Turtle Stone File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, Kumo Stone 01.jpg|Spider Mound File:Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, 07.jpg|First torii gate Katusragi-hitokotonushi-jinja keidaisha-2.jpg|Shrines within the grounds Katsuragi-hitokotonushi-jinja, Auxiliary Itokoto-Inari Shrine 01.jpg|Hitokoto Inari Shrine === Festivals === List of annual festivals held at Katsuragi Hitokotonushi Shrine. * New Year’s Day Festival (January 1) * Setsubun Festival (around February 3) * Spring Grand Festival (April 5) * Inari Shrine Festival (May 3) * Summer Purification Festival (June 30) * Autumn Grand Festival (Sacred Fire Festival) (September 15) * Zako Festival (second Sunday of October) * Ichiyō Raifuku Festival (winter solstice) === Appearances in works === * {{ill|lt=Matsuo Bashō|Q5676|WD=Q5676}} *Oi no Kobumi*{{Sfn|葛木坐一言主神社（神々）|1985年}} *: {{Cquote|{{small|As I made a pilgrimage through the land of Yamato and passed the foot of Mount Katsuragi, flowers were in full bloom on all sides, the peaks veiled in haze—at dawn the scenery was all the more alluring. Hearing people speak ill of the appearance of that god,}}
Still I longed to see—through flowers, the dawning face of the god|20px||*Oi no Kobumi*}} === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''Primary sources''' {{reflist|2|group="原"}} '''References''' {{reflist|2}} ===
